nativeRange.habitat: Marshes, rice paddies, ditches, and the muddy margins of slow-moving streams and ponds across temperate and tropical Asia. Highly tolerant of seasonal water-level fluctuation; grows both emersed (rounder marsh leaves) and submersed (narrow, often pink-red leaves) forms.

decorPreferences: open background or midground bush placement
bright lighting and iron dosing for pink-red coloration

summary: One of the most popular red-pink stem plants for planted aquariums. Forgiving and fast-growing, it serves as a background bush or trimmed midground hedge and colours pink-red under bright light with low nitrate and iron dosing.
careNotes: Rotala rotundifolia is among the most widely kept stem plants in the hobby and is generally considered one of the easiest red-pink stems to grow. Despite the Latin species name (which refers to the rounded leaves of the marsh-grown emersed form), the submerged aquarium form has narrow, needle-like leaves that range from yellow-green under low light to vivid pink and red under high light. Tropica describes the plant as relatively undemanding among Rotala species; Buce Plant, 2HR Aquarist, and aquariumbreeder.com all agree on a beginner-friendly profile. Sources differ on lighting requirement: Tropica rates light demand as 'low' (the plant will survive low light) while Buce Plant and aquariumbreeder.com recommend medium-to-high light (35-50 PAR) for the trademark pink-red coloration — averaged here to 'medium' as a sensible middle. CO2 is similarly contested: Tropica rates CO2 demand 'low', 2HR Aquarist notes the plant grows decently red without CO2 after acclimatisation, while Buce Plant lists CO2 as recommended for density; averaged to 'optional' since the plant unambiguously grows without CO2 but densifies and reddens faster with it. Temperature tolerance is broad — multiple sources cite 18-28 °C with the plant tolerating brief excursions to 17 °C in cooler tanks, but the comfortable range is 22-26 °C. pH 6.0-7.5 and soft-to-moderately-hard water are well tolerated. The famous deep pink-red colour is most reliably achieved by the 'nitrate limit' method: keep NO3 around 5-10 ppm while dosing iron and a full micronutrient mix; high-PAR lighting and CO2 amplify the effect. Many popular trade variants exist: 'Green' is the standard green-leaved form; 'Red' (also sold as 'Pink') is the most common pink-flushing form; 'Colorata' is a richly coloured cultivar; 'Blood Red' is a more demanding deep-red cultivar that wants high light and CO2; 'H'ra' has feathery pine-needle-like foliage with orange-pink tones; 'Orange Juice' is an orange-yellow morph. Important name confusion: 'Rotala indica' is sometimes used as a trade name for R. rotundifolia, but the true Rotala indica is a separate sister species (also covered in this database). Similarly, 'Rotala Vietnam' and 'Rotala wallichii' are related but distinct species, not variants of R. rotundifolia. Placement is background or trimmed-bush midground; the plant produces side shoots willingly and becomes bushier with frequent trimming. Lower leaves shade out and lose colour without regular pruning. Avoid drastic parameter swings — like other delicate stem plants, R. rotundifolia can melt if moved between very different water chemistries.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings — cut any portion of the stem (head cuttings of 10 cm or more work best) and replant directly into the substrate, where roots and side shoots develop quickly. Pinching off the apical bud encourages the mother stem to throw multiple lateral shoots, which can be trimmed off and replanted for rapid mass propagation. Floating cuttings will also root in the water column before planting. The plant can also be grown emersed for visually different (rounder-leaved) growth and is occasionally propagated this way in nurseries.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Used for species identity, family/origin (South-East Asia), height (15-30+ cm), growth rate (medium), light demand (low, with note that good light produces red leaves), CO2 demand (low), care difficulty (easy), placement (stem - side shoots, prune for compact form), and trade-name overlap with 'Rotala indica'. The supplied search URL (tropica.com/en/search?q=Rotala+rotundifolia) returned no per-species detail; resolved via search to the canonical product detail page id 4447.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Confirms family Lythraceae, origin South-East Asia, height 4-8" (~10-20 cm), pH 6.5-7.5, fast growth, easy-to-moderate care, background placement, high light + CO2 (20-30 ppm) for best red colour, propagation by stem cuttings, sensitivity to parameter swings (melt risk).

additional.name: 2HR Aquarist
additional.notes: Confirms easy care, hardy across wide parameter range, fast growth, grows reasonably red without CO2 but CO2 drives branching and density. Documents the 'nitrate limit' technique for driving deep red coloration (low NO3 + iron + micronutrients). Page returned 403 on direct WebFetch but search-result excerpt captured key claims.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (aquariumbreeder.com)
additional.notes: Cross-source confirmation of temperature 20-28 °C, pH 6.0-8.0, gH 2-12, lighting 35-50 PAR (medium-high), CO2 optional, height 5-30+ cm, propagation by head cuttings (≥10 cm) and runners, native countries (Japan, China, Taiwan, Laos, Vietnam, Thailand, India). Also documents R. rotundifolia vs R. indica species confusion.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op (R. rotundifolia 'H'ra' variant)
additional.notes: Documents the 'H'ra' cultivar: feathery pine-needle-like foliage, orange-pink coloration under high light + CO2, midground or background placement, creeping side-shoot growth. Used to enrich the trade-variant list in alsoKnownAs and careNotes.
